---
title: "Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR)"
subtitle: "book FFR Eng"
author: "謝慕揚 MD, PhD, FESC"
date: 2026-03-26
lang: zh-TW
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
number-sections: true
---

Comprehensive Lecture Notes
Compiled from Key Clinical Trials and Evidence
Mu-Yang Hsieh, MD, PhD, FESC
November 29, 2025
Contents
Theoretical Foundation
1.1
FFR Definition and Principles
. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1.1.1
Basic Definition
. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1.1.2
Theoretical Derivation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1.1.3
Clinical Significance . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Technical Aspects
2.1
Measurement Technique
. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 2.1.1
Equipment Requirements . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 2.1.2
Measurement Protocol . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 2.2
Non-Hyperemic Indices . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 2.2.1
iFR (Instantaneous Wave-free Ratio) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Wheatstone Bridge Principle
3.1
Application to Pressure Measurement
. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 3.1.1
Basic Bridge Structure . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 3.1.2
Application in FFR Systems . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 3.2
Technical Considerations . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . FAME 1 & FAME 2 Trials
4.1
FAME 1 (2009) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 4.2
FAME 2 (2012-2018) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Complete Revascularization in ACS
5.1
Landmark Trials in STEMI with Multivessel Disease . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 5.2
Timing of Complete Revascularization . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 5.3
Cardiogenic Shock Special Considerations . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 5.4
FFR-guided vs Angiography-guided Complete Revascularization . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 5.5
Meta-Analyses and Systematic Reviews . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Clinical Practice Guidelines Summary
6.1
Current Guideline Recommendations . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 6.2
FFR/iFR Usage Recommendations . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Controversies and Future Directions
7.1
Current Controversies . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 7.2
Future Research Directions . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Conclusions


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
Theoretical Foundation
1.1
FFR Definition and Principles
Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) is a physiological index used to assess the hemodynamic significance of coronary
artery stenosis. 1.1.1
Basic Definition
FFR = Pd
Pa
(1)
Where:
• Pd = Distal coronary pressure (pressure distal to stenosis)
• Pa = Aortic pressure (proximal pressure)
1.1.2
Theoretical Derivation
FFR represents the ratio of maximum achievable blood flow in the presence of stenosis to the theoretical max-
imum flow without stenosis in the myocardial territory supplied by that coronary artery. According to Poiseuille’s law, blood flow is proportional to the pressure gradient:
Q = ∆P
R
(2)
Under maximal hyperemia (induced by adenosine):
FFR = Qmax,stenosis
Qmax,normal
= (Pd −Pv)/Rmyo
(Pa −Pv)/Rmyo
≈Pd
Pa
(3)
When Pv (central venous pressure) is much smaller than Pa and Pd, this simplifies to FFR = Pd/Pa. 1.1.3
Clinical Significance
Table 1: Clinical Interpretation of FFR Values
FFR Value
Clinical Significance
FFR > 0.80
No hemodynamic significance, PCI not recommended
FFR ≤0.80
Hemodynamically significant, PCI recommended
FFR < 0.75
Definite ischemia, strong recommendation for intervention
0.75 < FFR <
0.80
Gray zone, requires clinical judgment
FFR < 0.50
Severe ischemia, high-risk lesion
Technical Aspects
2.1
Measurement Technique
2.1.1
Equipment Requirements
• Pressure wire
• Hyperemic agents: Adenosine (140-180 µg/kg/min IV) or IC bolus (40-80 µg)
• Pressure measurement system
• Angiography equipment


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
2.1.2
Measurement Protocol
Table 2: Standard FFR Measurement Procedure
Step
Operational Details
Calibration: Zero the pressure wire and measurement system
Equalization: Confirm Pd = Pa at guide catheter position
Wire advancement: Advance pressure wire distal to lesion
Induce hyperemia: Administer adenosine for maximal hyperemia
Record pressure: Record Pd and Pa during hyperemia
Calculate FFR: Automatic or manual calculation of FFR = Pd/Pa
Pullback verification: Withdraw wire to guide catheter to check for drift
2.2
Non-Hyperemic Indices
2.2.1
iFR (Instantaneous Wave-free Ratio)
iFR is measured during a specific period of the cardiac cycle (wave-free period) without hyperemic agents:
• Measurement timing: Mid-diastole (approximately 25% of cardiac cycle)
• Cutoff: iFR ≤0.89 considered significant
• Advantages: No adenosine required, shorter procedure time, fewer side effects
• Correlation with FFR: Approximately 80-85% concordance
Wheatstone Bridge Principle
3.1
Application to Pressure Measurement
Pressure wire systems utilize the Wheatstone Bridge principle to convert mechanical pressure into electrical
signals. 3.1.1
Basic Bridge Structure
The bridge consists of four resistors, with at least one being a pressure-sensitive resistor (piezoresistive sensor):
Vout = Vin
(
R3
R3 + R4
−
R2
R1 + R2
)
(4)
When pressure changes, the resistance of the pressure-sensitive element changes, causing a voltage output
variation that allows pressure measurement. 3.1.2
Application in FFR Systems
Table 3: Wheatstone Bridge Components in FFR Systems
Component
Function
Pressure sensor
Located at wire tip, contains piezoresistive elements
Signal conversion
Converts pressure changes to voltage changes
Amplifier
Amplifies minute voltage signals
Calibration system
Eliminates temperature, drift, and other interference
Digital conversion
Converts analog signals to digital pressure readings


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
3.2
Technical Considerations
• Drift: Prolonged measurements may cause signal drift; calibration before and after is essential
• Temperature compensation: Body temperature variations can affect resistance values
• Sensitivity: Modern pressure wires can detect pressure changes within ±2 mmHg


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
FAME 1 & FAME 2 Trials
4.1
FAME 1 (2009)
Table 4: FAME 1 Trial Detailed Information
Category
Details
Full Name
Fractional Flow Reserve versus Angiography for Multivessel
Evaluation
Publication Year
2009
Journal
New England Journal of Medicine
Study Design
Multicenter RCT
Sample Size
1,005 patients
Inclusion Criteria
Multivessel coronary artery disease suitable for PCI
Exclusion Criteria
STEMI <5 days, history of CABG
Intervention
FFR arm: Treat only lesions with FFR ≤0.80
Angio arm: Treat all lesions with diameter stenosis ≥50%
Primary Endpoint
1-year MACE (death, MI, repeat revascularization)
Primary Results
FFR arm MACE: 13.2% vs Angio arm 18.3% (P=0.02)
HR: 0.72 (95% CI: 0.54-0.96)
Secondary Results
FFR arm used fewer stents (1.9 vs 2.7, P<0.001)
Similar procedure time
Similar contrast volume
5-Year Follow-up
MACE: 28% (FFR) vs 31% (Angio), P=0.31
FFR arm significantly better in first 2 years, similar from 2-5
years
4.2
FAME 2 (2012-2018)
Table 5: FAME 2 Trial Comprehensive Analysis
Category
Details
Full Name
Fractional Flow Reserve-Guided PCI versus Medical Therapy in
Stable Coronary Disease
Publication Year
2012 (initial), 2018 (5-year)
Journal
New England Journal of Medicine
Study Design
Multicenter RCT
Sample Size
1,220 patients with FFR ≤0.80
Inclusion Criteria
Stable CAD with at least one stenosis and FFR ≤0.80
Randomization
PCI + OMT arm: 888 patients
OMT only arm: 332 patients
Registry (FFR >0.80): 166 patients


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
Table 5 – continued
Category
Details
Primary Endpoint
(2-year)
Death, MI, or urgent revascularization
PCI + OMT: 4.3% vs OMT only: 12.7%
HR: 0.32 (95% CI: 0.19-0.53), P<0.001
Components
Death: 0.9% vs 1.5% (P=0.49)
MI: 3.4% vs 3.4% (P=0.98)
Urgent revascularization: 1.6% vs 11.1% (P<0.001)
5-Year Results
Primary endpoint: 13.9% vs 27.0%
HR: 0.46 (95% CI: 0.34-0.63), P<0.001
Death or MI: 8.1% vs 12.0%
HR: 0.66 (95% CI: 0.43-1.00), P=0.05
Registry Patients
FFR >0.80 with medical therapy only
5-year event rate: 9.0%
Lower than both randomized arms
Key Message
FFR-guided PCI significantly reduces urgent revascularization
Trend toward reduced death/MI at 5 years
Validates FFR ≤0.80 as intervention threshold


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
Complete Revascularization in ACS
5.1
Landmark Trials in STEMI with Multivessel Disease
Table 6: Major Complete Revascularization Trials in STEMI
Trial
Journal/Year
N
Primary Findings
PRAMI
NEJM 2013
Preventive PCI reduced composite
endpoint
HR 0.35 (0.21-0.58), P<0.001
First to show benefit
CvLPRIT
JACC 2015
Complete revasc reduced MACE
HR 0.45 (0.24-0.84), P=0.009
Confirmed PRAMI findings
COMPLETE
NEJM 2019
4,041
Largest trial
Primary EP: CV death/MI
HR 0.74 (0.60-0.91), P=0.004
CV death/MI: 7.8% vs 10.5% at 3
years
Guideline-changing evidence
MULTISTARS AMI
NEJM 2023
Immediate vs Staged complete revasc
Primary EP: 8.5% vs 7.3%
HR 1.15 (0.73-1.82)
Non-inferiority proven
FULL REVASC
NEJM 2024
1,542
FFR-guided complete vs Culprit-only
4.8-year follow-up
Primary EP: 19.0% vs 20.4%
HR 0.93 (0.74-1.16), P=0.51
Neutral result
FIRE
NEJM 2023
1,445
Physiology-guided complete vs
Culprit-only (≥75 years)
CV death/MI/revasc: 23.3% vs 30.0%
HR 0.73 (0.58-0.90), P=0.004
Benefit in elderly
5.2
Timing of Complete Revascularization
Table 7: Immediate vs Staged Complete Revascularization
Trial
Journal/Year
N
Key Findings
COMPLETE Timing
NEJM 2019
4,041
In-hospital staged vs Post-discharge
staged
Similar outcomes, both superior to
culprit-only
MULTISTARS AMI
NEJM 2023
Immediate vs Staged (19-45 days)
Non-inferiority proven
Staged approach acceptable


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
Table 7 – continued
Trial
Journal/Year
N
Key Findings
SMILE
JACC 2016
Single-stage vs Multi-stage
(in-hospital)
Single-stage superior
HR 0.55 (0.36-0.83), P=0.004
5.3
Cardiogenic Shock Special Considerations
Table 8: Revascularization Strategy in STEMI with Cardiogenic
Shock
Trial
Journal/Year
N
Primary Results and Clinical
Implications
CULPRIT-
SHOCK
NEJM 2017
30 days:
Death/RRT: 45.9% (culprit-only) vs
55.4% (MV-PCI)
RR 0.83 (0.71-0.96), P=0.01
Mortality: 43.3% vs 51.6%, P=0.03
1 year:
Mortality: 50.0% vs 56.9% (P=0.07)
HF rehospitalization: 4.5% vs 0.9%
(P=0.01)
Landmark trial: Changed guideline
recommendations
Culprit-only preferred in cardiogenic
shock
Consider staged revascularization
5.4
FFR-guided vs Angiography-guided Complete Revascularization
Table 9: Comparison of FFR and Angiography-Guided Complete
Revascularization
Trial
Journal/Year
N
Main Results
DANAMI-3
PRIMULTI
Lancet 2015
FFR-guided reduced PCI need
Approximately 50% lesions FFR>0.80
MACE improved
COMPARE-ACUTE
NEJM 2017
FFR-guided immediate complete
Primary EP: HR 0.35 (P<0.001)
Supports FFR-guided strategy
FLOWER-MI
NEJM 2021
1,171
FFR non-superior to angio
Primary EP: 5.5% vs 4.2%
HR 1.32 (0.78-2.23), P=0.31
Stent use: 1.01 vs 1.50


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
Table 9 – continued
Trial
Journal/Year
N
Main Results
FRAME-AMI
European Heart
J
FFR-guided superior to angio
FULL REVASC
NEJM 2024
1,542
FFR-guided vs culprit-only
4.8-year follow-up: neutral result
HR 0.93 (0.74-1.16), P=0.51


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
5.5
Meta-Analyses and Systematic Reviews
Table 10: Important Meta-Analyses of Multivessel PCI in ACS
Meta-analysis
Journal/Year
Main Conclusions
Elgendy et al. JACC Cardiovasc
Interv 2017
8 RCTs, 7,030 patients included
Complete revasc significantly reduced CV
death and MI
OR 0.74 (0.61-0.89)
Pavasini et al. Eur Heart J 2020
9 RCTs included
Complete revasc reduced CV death
HR 0.72 (0.58-0.89)
Biscaglia et al. PLOS ONE 2024
8 RCTs, 4,433 patients
FFR vs angio-guided analysis
MACE: RR 0.69 (0.52-0.90)
ACS subgroup: RR 0.77 (0.51-1.17)


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
Clinical Practice Guidelines Summary
6.1
Current Guideline Recommendations
Table 11: 2024 ACS Multivessel Disease Management Guidelines
Clinical Scenario
Recommendations
STEMI + MVD (no
shock)
Class I: Complete revascularization recommended
Can be immediate or staged (in-hospital or post-discharge)
Consider FFR/iFR guidance for non-culprit lesions
STEMI + MVD + Shock
Class I: Culprit-only PCI
Class IIa: Consider staged revascularization
Based on CULPRIT-SHOCK trial
NSTEMI + MVD
Class IIa: Complete revascularization
Staged approach more common than immediate
Can use FFR/iFR to assess non-culprit lesions
Elderly Patients (≥75
years)
Based on FIRE trial
Physiology-guided complete revasc beneficial
Assess surgical risk and benefit
Presence of CTO
STEMI stable patients: Immediate CTO PCI not recommended
Cardiogenic shock: CTO should not be treated immediately
Consider subsequent staged intervention
6.2
FFR/iFR Usage Recommendations
Table 12: Application of Physiologic Assessment in ACS
Scenario
Recommendations and Evidence Level
Stable CAD multivessel disease
Class I, Level A
FFR ≤0.80 recommend PCI
FFR > 0.80 can safely defer
STEMI non-culprit lesions
Class IIa, Level B
Can use FFR/iFR for assessment
Helps decide immediate or staged PCI
NSTEMI non-culprit lesions
Class IIa, Level B
FFR/iFR helps reduce unnecessary PCI
Safe and effective compared to angio-guided
Timing of measurement (ACS)
Index procedure or staged (days later) both acceptable
Studies show FFR/iFR values stable
iFR may be more suitable in acute phase (no adenosine
needed)
Not recommended
Culprit lesion (clearly needs immediate PCI)
Cardiogenic shock (CULPRIT-SHOCK)
STEMI with left main disease


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
Controversies and Future Directions
7.1
Current Controversies
Table 13: Controversial Issues in FFR and Complete Revascular-
ization
Controversy
Discussion
FLOWER-MI neutral results
FFR-guided failed to prove superiority over angio-guided
Possible reasons: Modern angio assessment already accurate,
low event rate, trial design
Does not mean FFR is useless; both methods acceptable
Timing of complete revasc
Immediate vs staged still debated
MULTISTARS AMI showed non-inferiority
Immediate may increase contrast volume, procedural
complexity
Staged requires rehospitalization, increases healthcare costs
FFR accuracy in ACS
Microvascular dysfunction may affect accuracy
Most studies show good reliability
iFR may be alternative option
FAME 2 soft endpoint issue
Main benefit from reduced urgent revascularization
No significant difference in death/MI (trend at 5 years)
Non-blinded design may introduce bias
Cost-effectiveness
FFR equipment cost vs reduced unnecessary PCI
Long-term follow-up shows cost-effective
Requires additional training and equipment
7.2
Future Research Directions
• Integration of imaging and physiology: Combined IVUS/OCT + FFR assessment
• Novel physiologic indices: dPR, RFR, virtual FFR (computed FFR)
• Artificial intelligence applications: AI-assisted FFR interpretation, outcome prediction
• Special populations: Optimal strategies for diabetes, chronic kidney disease, very elderly patients
• Long-term outcomes: >5-year follow-up of complete revascularization effects
• Precision medicine: Gene and biomarker-guided personalized treatment


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) Lecture Notes
Conclusions
FFR represents a major advancement in percutaneous coronary intervention, providing an objective physiolog-
ical assessment standard. Key conclusions include:
1. Stable CAD: FFR-guided PCI superior to pure angiography-guided approach (FAME 1 & 2)
2. STEMI with multivessel disease: Complete revascularization superior to culprit-only (COMPLETE and
others)
3. FFR in ACS: Safe to use for non-culprit lesion assessment
4. Cardiogenic shock: Culprit-only is preferred strategy (CULPRIT-SHOCK)
5. Timing choice: Both immediate and staged complete revascularization are acceptable
6. Elderly patients: Physiology-guided approach still beneficial (FIRE)
The application of FFR and iFR should be integrated with clinical judgment, imaging assessment, and patient
characteristics to achieve optimal personalized treatment strategies.
